properties.raw_text:
Nightglass
Visual: A Thunderbolt stealth bomber with black resonance-null plating, flattened engine housings, and bomb bays hidden behind seamless hull panels. Its exhaust appears as a faint distortion rather than flame.
Behavior: Stealth bomber and command-killer. It sacrifices payload volume for psionic shielding, sensor masking, silent approach, and precision resonance charges that overload command bunkers, shield nodes, or unaware Mim clusters. Halix rarely admits these aircraft exist.
Blackbox Fragment: “Radar saw weather. Thermal saw nothing. The bunker opened from the inside out.”

properties.raw_text:
Ghostmark
Visual: A modified Razor Skimmer. A narrow black recon craft with psionic shielding, adaptive cloak plating, muffled grav vanes, and needle-like sensors folded beneath its prow. Invisible to the eyes and nearly undetectable to instruments.
Behavior: Recon, target marking, and assassination skimmer. It avoids direct combat, instead identifying command units, artillery nests, or shield weak points. Once it marks a target, Halix strike assets usually arrive before the enemy knows it has been seen.

properties.raw_text:
Jackknife
Visual: A modified Razor Skimmer. A jagged grav-raider built from stolen plates and exposed thruster vanes, with grappling harpoons mounted beneath the prow and boarding hooks folded along the flanks.
Behavior: Boarding skimmer and ambush craft. It closes too fast, fires grapples into vehicles or low-flying craft, then drags itself into blind spots where cutters can breach armor. Fragile in clean battlefield conditions, but vicious in wreck fields, asteroid docks, and station approaches.
Pirate Vox: “Do not chase the Jackknife. That is how it gets behind you.”

properties.raw_text:
Grabhook Mule
Visual: A modified Mule Carrier. A battered boarding transport with heavy clamp-arms, hull magnets, breaching collars, and welded cage racks along the exterior. Its rear ramp is reinforced like a siege door.
Behavior: Boarding carrier and forced-entry hauler. The Grabhook latches onto ships, bunkers, or disabled vehicles, locks itself in place, and burns a passage open while its crew prepares to storm through. It can also rip cargo containers or escape pods directly from a target.
Boarder Vox: “The ramp does not open until the door on the other side stops being a door.”

Boarder Vox: “The ramp does not open until the door on the other side stops being a door.”

properties.raw_text: Merchant-Lord - A ruling magnate of the Luthan League whose authority comes from ownership, debt, shipping tonnage, contracts, and the ability to fund violence. A Merchant-Lord is not simply a noble with money. They are a sovereign creditor, fleet patron, arms buyer, and political shareholder whose wars are fought through hired crews, leased frames, insurance clauses, and debt enforcement. In Luthan space, a Merchant-Lord’s command may look like a business decision, but refusal can bankrupt worlds.

properties.raw_text: Forge-Lord - A Karnate industrial ruler who commands foundries, labor pools, war engines, and military production lines. Forge-Lords measure power in furnaces, output, conscripts, and the number of walkers they can keep armed. Their rule is practical and brutal: ore goes in, weapons come out, and people are another form of fuel. To serve a Forge-Lord is to be part of the machine, whether as engineer, soldier, slave, or tribute.

properties.raw_text: Foundry-Clan - A Karnate political-industrial house built around a major forge, refinery, shipyard, or war-production complex. Foundry-Clans are family, corporation, military command, and prison system fused together. Each clan owes tribute to the broader Karnate war machine while competing against rival clans for contracts, resources, and prestige. Their identities are carried in furnace marks, slag banners, production codes, and the distinctive scars of their labor forces.

properties.raw_text: Belt-King - A pirate sovereign of the Orak Belt Syndicates whose rule depends on reputation, loot, fear, and the ability to keep raider crews pointed outward instead of at each other. Belt-Kings rarely hold stable territory in the formal sense. They rule flotillas, stations, salvage cradles, black markets, and oath-bound captains. A Belt-King is obeyed while profitable, feared while victorious, and replaced the moment weakness becomes visible.

properties.raw_text: Crown Council - The ruling assembly of the Crown Concordant, composed of hereditary sovereigns, major noble houses, and knightly power blocs. The Council frames its authority as lawful inheritance, sacred duty, and aristocratic stewardship, but much of its real work is negotiation, rivalry, marriage politics, and disputes over legitimacy. Council decisions carry the weight of royal command, though every decree is filtered through house pride, old feuds, and battlefield honor.

properties.raw_text: Noble House - A hereditary political lineage that controls land, ships, military obligations, retainers, and often its own mech traditions. In Crown and Cindral cultures, a noble house is not just a family name. It is a legal institution, military command, patronage network, and symbol people may live or die for. Houses preserve their authority through banners, oaths, arranged alliances, champion pilots, inherited frames, and public acts of honor or vengeance.

properties.raw_text: Guild databank consensus identifies the War as the most widely accepted explanation for the empty stars: why every expansion beyond Primus finds only dead orbital works, shattered megastructures, and ruins belonging to civilizations whose names, bodies, and purposes remain unknowable. No coherent record of the War survives, only ruin-fragments dated to before the first life walked on Primus, contradictory whispers taken from captured Mim utterances, and the continuing existence of the entity catalogued as The Being. Whether the War was a conflict, a purge, a failed defense, or something with no human equivalent remains unresolved, and all Directorate personnel are advised to treat certainty on the matter as cult behavior, enemy influence, or academic fraud.

properties.raw_text: A Guild-manufactured faster-than-light drive that forces a vessel into the Manifold, where it follows narrow, charted corridors of temporarily stable space. Each engine requires a classified fuel source and constant course correction against the Manifold’s shifting geometry; deviation from an approved lane can strand, destroy, or scatter a ship across unknown space. The Guild alone produces, licenses, and maintains Manifold Engines.
properties.description: A Guild-manufactured faster-than-light drive that forces a vessel into the Manifold, where it follows narrow, charted corridors of stable space. Each engine requires a classified fuel source and constant course correction against the Manifold’s shifting geometry; deviation from an approved lane can strand, destroy, or scatter a ship across unknown space. The Guild alone is responsible enough to produce, license, and maintain Manifold Engines.

properties.raw_text:
The Pelagos Union was a democratic federation of ocean worlds, floating cities, orbital shipyards, and agricultural habitats. Its economy was built around aquaculture, biological manufacturing, medicine, shipping, and the export of food to less habitable systems.
Political authority was divided between planetary assemblies, trade unions, naval districts, and habitat councils. Pelagos possessed a powerful defensive fleet but had little interest in territorial expansion.

QUARANTINE ENACTED

properties.raw_text: A permanent Voidborn breach occupying the former heart of the Damasine Hegemony, created during the twenty seven year war that destroyed the empire and rendered its surviving territory inaccessible. The Hegemony’s final fleet attempted to collapse the original fracture through a massed dimensional resonance detonation, but the breach survived and may have expanded beyond reliable observation. No stable systems are believed to remain within the lost region, and reports indicate that matter, energy, and local geometry continue to degrade around its boundaries. All Guild lanes approaching the Rift have been severed, navigational records purged, and unauthorized transit classified as deliberate entry into a Class IV erasure zone.

properties.raw_text:
The Damasine Hegemony was an authoritarian naval empire controlling a long chain of industrial and agricultural systems. Provincial rulers were permitted considerable autonomy so long as they provided taxes, conscripts, and warships to the central throne.
Damasine expansion was aggressive but conventional. Its fleets were experienced, heavily armored, and supported by fortress systems capable of sustaining years of siege.
PERMANENT VOIDBORN RIFT CONFIRMED 
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Ilyr Consortium was a commercial alliance of mining corporations, colony governments, transport families, and worker-owned extraction syndicates. It controlled resource-rich systems but lacked the centralized political identity of the major human powers.
Ilyr settlements ranged from wealthy metropolitan worlds to isolated mines and industrial habitats. The Consortium’s principal achievement was maintaining trade between communities that otherwise shared little culture or law.
SECTORS CONSUMED BY STELLAR MAW

properties.raw_text: Psionics - Rare cognitive phenomena first documented in populations with prolonged exposure to resonance. Unlike resonance, psionics arise from an energy field generated by an unknown mechanism within the brain. Current theories posit that if resonance is energy derived from the Crucible, perhaps psionics are derived from a linkage to another plane of reality as well. Natural psionics are exceptionally uncommon, though artificial induction through resonance exposure and/or neural modification has produced additional subjects, often at the cost of permanent neurological damage, psychosis, or death. No consensus exists on why resonance exposure sometimes awakens psionic abilities, and the relationship between the two remains one of the greatest unsolved mysteries in modern science.
properties.body:
Psionics
Rare cognitive phenomena first documented in populations with prolonged exposure to resonance. Unlike resonance, psionics arise from an energy field generated by an unknown mechanism within the brain. Current theories posit that if resonance is energy derived from the Crucible, perhaps psionics are derived from a linkage to another plane of reality as well. Natural psionics are exceptionally uncommon, though artificial induction through resonance exposure and/or neural modification has produced additional subjects, often at the cost of permanent neurological damage, psychosis, or death. No consensus exists on why resonance exposure sometimes awakens psionic abilities, and the relationship between the two remains one of the greatest unsolved mysteries in modern science.
properties.tags_text: Psionics, Psychic, Psionic, Resonance, Crucible
properties.name: Psionics

properties.raw_text:
The Asterion Assembly was a federal republic formed from six industrial systems and dozens of lesser orbital municipalities. Its elected Chambers were dominated by shipwright unions, civil engineers, colonial delegates, and fleet officers, while local governments retained broad authority over taxation, policing, and settlement law. Asterion vessels were known for reliability rather than elegance, and its navy was among the largest forces outside direct Guild control.
The Assembly maintained cordial relations with the Crown and Luthan League but resisted permanent dependence upon either. At its height, it governed nearly forty billion citizens and operated an independent network of hospitals, schools, shipyards, agricultural habitats, and defensive stations.
SECTORS OVERRUN WITH MIMS
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Tyren Ascendancy was a hereditary officer-state formed after a coalition of colonial armies overthrew several failing corporate administrations. Its ruling dynasties controlled the fleet and central ministries, while provincial governors managed extensive agricultural, industrial, and commercial territories.
Tyren culture placed high value on discipline, lineage, military education, and service to the state. Despite its authoritarian rule, the Ascendancy maintained functioning courts, universities, and a substantial civilian economy.
SECTORS CONTROLLED BY CEREBRAX
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Uldren Combine was an industrial federation governed by corporate boards, labor councils, military administrators, and planetary production ministries. It controlled rich mineral systems and maintained enormous foundry fleets capable of constructing stations, warships, and habitation cylinders without Guild assistance.
Although unequal and politically severe, Uldren offered stable employment, reliable food distribution, and considerable local autonomy to productive worlds. Its influence rested on manufacturing capacity rather than ideology or cultural prestige.
SECTORS OVERRUN WITH MIMS
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Serevine Republic was a prosperous parliamentary state centered on manufacturing worlds, industrious cities, and heavily populated orbital habitats. Its civil service was respected for competence, while a large professional navy protected commercial routes linking its inner systems to independent frontier settlements.
Serevine politics were competitive and often bitter, but its parties agreed on gradual expansion, public infrastructure, and maintaining relative independence from both the Guild and the major human powers. Its citizens generally regarded the Republic as stable, wealthy, and unlikely to suffer the failures seen in harsher frontier states.
QUARANTINE IN EFFECT
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Tervan Covenant was a defensive compact of agricultural kingdoms, mining republics, and old colonial fortress-worlds. Its members shared a navy and foreign policy but retained separate legal systems, noble houses, elected councils, and religious institutions. Tervan society valued military service, land stewardship, and regional customs.
Tervan was neither technologically exceptional nor politically unified. Its strength came from deep reserves, fortified population centers, experienced officers, and a citizenry accustomed to long campaigns against pirates and neighboring states.
EXTREME TEMPORAL INSTABILITY DETECTED IN FORMER SECTORS
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Vannic Commonwealth was a parliamentary federation of old colony worlds, agricultural systems, industrial cities, and independent orbital settlements. It was politically moderate, economically stable, and known for strong municipal governments rather than grand ideological projects.
Its outer systems had suffered from declining stellar output, forcing expensive heating programs and periodic population transfers. Despite this, the Commonwealth remained prosperous and maintained close commercial relations with several surviving powers.
SYSTEMS ANNIHILATED DUE TO BASILICA

properties.raw_text:
The Taloran Directorate was a centralized state governed by civil ministries, military prefectures, and a powerful engineering corps. It controlled heavily developed systems with dense orbital infrastructure, planned cities, artificial habitats, and large-scale public works.
Taloran society was orderly, technically competent, and politically rigid. Its legitimacy rested upon the promise that the state could maintain transportation, power, food distribution, and physical security under any conditions.
GRAVITATIONAL PHYSICS UNSTABLE DUE TO SHEPHERD INTERFERENCE 
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Aurelian Synod was a federation of secular governments and established religious institutions. It governed pilgrimage worlds, commercial stations, agricultural colonies, and old cultural centers whose traditions predated the modern Guild lane network.
The Synod was known for diplomacy, historical scholarship, public ritual, and its willingness to absorb refugees from neighboring conflicts. It maintained a capable navy but preferred mediation to warfare.
NEBULITE ASSIMILATION CONFIRMED
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
The Nacre Union was a loose confederation of manufacturing worlds known for ceramics, structural composites, optical systems, consumer technology, and precision machinery. Its cities were built around cooperative workshops and regional production houses rather than centralized corporations.
Nacre politics were local, argumentative, and slow. The Union rarely projected military power beyond its territory, but its defense fleets were numerous and its population deeply committed to protecting their home systems.
PHAGE INFESTATION DETECTED
QUARANTINE ENACTED
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.raw_text:
Needle Interceptor
Visual: Narrow spindle-shaped craft with recessed engines, dark sensor plating, and an oversized communications array along its spine.
Behavior: Long-range scout and courier. Observes enemy movements, carries priority dispatches, and withdraws before heavier ships can respond.
Dispatch Log: “Contact confirmed beyond the lane marker. The Needle delivered its report six hours before our own sensors found them.”

properties.raw_text:
Lantern Corvette
Visual: Lean hull dominated by folding sensor masts and luminous detection vanes. Weapon mounts are small and widely spaced.
Behavior: Fleet picket and reconnaissance ship. Detects ambushes, surveys unstable lanes, and extends the sensor range of larger formations.
Picket Report: “Lantern Three saw the raiders before they cleared the debris field. The convoy had already turned by the time they opened fire.”

Viper Corvette
Visual: Low-profile hull with angular heat sinks and paired torpedo tubes running nearly its entire length.
Behavior: Torpedo ambusher. Hides near stations, debris fields, or lane exits before striking larger vessels at close range.
Blackbox Transcript: “No drive flare. No targeting lock. Just two torpedoes appearing out of the wreckage beneath us.”

Aegis Frigate
Visual: Broad wedge-shaped hull surrounded by clustered point-defense turrets. Its armor is thin except around engines and ammunition stores.
Behavior: Dedicated fleet screen. Intercepts missiles, drones, and strike craft before they can reach carriers or capital ships.
Fleet Report: “The sky between us and the carrier became solid gunfire. Nothing larger than scrap reached the flight deck.”

properties.raw_text:
Veil Frigate
Visual: Smooth dark hull broken by antenna ridges and rotating resonance dishes. Its engine wake disperses into a faint haze rather than a bright trail.
Behavior: Electronic warfare frigate. Scrambles targeting systems, creates false contacts, and conceals allied movements behind sensor interference.
Sensor Log: “Tracking identified twelve cruisers. Visual confirmation found one frigate and empty space.”

properties.raw_text:
Harpoon Frigate
Visual: Heavy forward hull fitted with grappling launchers, breaching clamps, and armored boarding tubes.
Behavior: Capture and interdiction vessel. Disables engines, anchors fleeing ships, and delivers boarding teams into crippled targets.
Boarding Report: “The clamps struck before the engines finished dying. Forty seconds later, their marines were cutting through the inner hatch.”

properties.raw_text:
Longbow Cruiser
Visual: Slender cruiser built around a spinal resonance lance. Auxiliary gun decks sit far behind the reinforced prow.
Behavior: Long-range artillery ship. Cripples priority targets before the main battle line closes, but performs poorly when surrounded.
Gunnery Record: “Range control called the shot impossible. The Longbow fired anyway. Enemy drive output ceased eight seconds later.”

properties.raw_text:
Manticore Cruiser
Visual: Thick-backed hull covered in sealed missile cells. Its armored flanks unfold into layered launch racks during combat.
Behavior: Missile cruiser. Saturates formations with guided ordnance and forces enemy point defenses to choose which threats to stop.
Defense Analysis: “Point defense eliminated four hundred contacts. The remaining eighty-seven were enough.”

Citadel Cruiser
Visual: Tall central superstructure surrounded by armored command decks, communications towers, and projecting resonance vanes.
Behavior: Fleet coordination vessel. Directs squadrons, strengthens communications, and maintains formation cohesion during confused engagements.
Command Log: “Comms failed across the formation, but every ship still received the Citadel clearly. The line never broke.”

Visual: Blunt armored carrier with forward-opening hangars and rows of atmospheric landing craft secured beneath its hull.
Behavior: Planetary assault carrier. Delivers mechs, troops, and ground vehicles directly into defended landing zones.
Landing Report: “The prow entered the atmosphere under fire. Its first mech reached the ground before the carrier finished burning through the clouds.”

Atlas Support Carrier
Visual: Bulky segmented hull carrying detachable fuel tanks, ammunition vaults, and replacement components beneath an armored spine.
Behavior: Fleet replenishment ship. Refuels vessels, replaces expended ordnance, and keeps extended campaigns supplied away from established ports.
Logistics Record: “The fleet arrived empty. Six hours beside the Atlas and every magazine, tank, and launch rack was full again.”

properties.raw_text:
Eclipse Battlecruiser
Visual: Long predatory hull with oversized engines and weapon batteries concentrated around its narrow prow.
Behavior: Capital hunter. Uses superior speed to isolate cruisers and carriers, destroys them quickly, and disengages before dreadnoughts can respond.
Tactical Report: “The Eclipse crossed our firing line, killed the carrier, and was already outside lance range when the escorts turned.”

properties.raw_text:
Monolith Dreadnought
Visual: Enormous slab-sided vessel surrounded by layered armor belts, shield emitters, and dense point-defense towers.
Behavior: Defensive fleet anchor. Protects nearby vessels, absorbs concentrated fire, and turns strategically important positions into nearly immovable fortresses.
Fleet Record: “We spent nine hours trying to move it from the gate. The Monolith did not alter course by a single degree.”

properties.raw_text:
Cistern Freighter
Visual: A reinforced central spine surrounded by rows of spherical storage tanks. Emergency bulkheads divide the vessel into dozens of sealed sections.
Behavior: Transports fuel, water, industrial gases, and other volatile cargo between systems. Commonly travels in heavily guarded convoys.
Convoy Report: “One tank ruptured and froze half the hull. The remaining compartments held long enough to save the colony.”

properties.raw_text:
Wayfarer Liner
Visual: Long utilitarian hull lined with compact habitation blocks and rotating gravity sections. Exterior decoration is limited to registry markings and destination banners.
Behavior: Mass passenger and colony transport. Carries thousands in austere conditions on routes too dangerous or unprofitable for luxury liners.
Passenger Account: “There were twelve of us to a compartment and one window on the entire deck. Nobody complained when the new sun appeared.”

Wrecker Barge
Visual: Open-framed vessel fitted with cutting arms, magnetic cranes, storage cages, and clamps large enough to hold broken warships.
Behavior: Salvage and recovery platform. Retrieves wrecks, stranded mechs, and damaged vessels from battlefields before pirates or scavengers arrive.
Salvage Log: “The cruiser was split through the middle. The Wrecker brought back both halves and sixteen survivors.”

Prospector Barge
Visual: Industrial hull wrapped around drills, refinery drums, ore grinders, and detachable mining craft.
Behavior: Mobile extraction platform. Mines asteroids and small moons, processes raw material, and supplies isolated colonies or fleet foundries.
Mining Record: “The rock entered the forward grinders at dawn. By the next shift, the refinery was loading usable alloy.”

properties.raw_text:
The Hadran Polity was a bureaucratic federation of densely populated systems governed through elected ministries, regional administrations, courts, and a large professional civil service. It maintained extensive welfare programs, standardized infrastructure, and a modest but modern defensive fleet.
Hadran law was complicated but stable. Its greatest political conflicts concerned taxation, regional autonomy, industrial policy, and the limits of emergency authority.
MEMETIC QUARANTINE ENACTED
LANE ACCESS REVOKED

properties.name: Hadran
Ferrith War
properties.description: The Luthan League attempted to impose standardized tariffs and inspection rights on Kestrel merchant routes near Ferrith. Fighting centered on convoy seizures, station blockades, and attacks on customs platforms rather than planetary invasion. The settlement granted the League access to Ferrith while preserving Kestrel control of several independent trade routes.

properties.name: Deyros War
properties.description: Repeated Orak raids against Crown settlements escalated into a sustained campaign across Deyros and Harrier Field. Crown forces captured several pirate anchorages but could not destroy the mobile syndicate fleets. The conflict established the fortified patrol network that still defines the region.

properties.name: Braxis Border War
properties.description: Damasine expansion crossed into systems claimed by the Cindral March, leading to six years of fleet actions and fortress sieges. Cindral forces held Braxis Hold but lost several outer colonies. The resulting border remained heavily militarized until the destruction of the Hegemony.

properties.name: Pelagos Trade War
properties.description: The conflict began over League attempts to control food exports and shipping insurance from Pelagos territory. Both sides targeted merchant fleets, ports, and bonded cargo rather than civilian populations. Pelagos retained commercial independence but granted League firms preferred access to several major markets.

properties.name: Shivra War
properties.description: Karnate forces attempted to seize mineral systems supplying Uldren heavy industry. The war became a prolonged contest between Karnate assault fleets and Uldren’s ability to replace ships faster than they could be destroyed. Karnate secured the Shivra Belt but failed to break the wider Combine.

properties.name: Veyra Corridor War
properties.description: Disputes over pilgrimage routes, shrine jurisdiction, and the taxation of religious traffic escalated into armed convoy escorts and station seizures. Neither faction attempted to conquer the other’s core worlds. The final agreement established separate Aurelian and Veyran pilgrimage administrations along the corridor.

properties.name: Taloran-Halix Border War
properties.description: The Directorate objected to Halix sensor stations operating near Taloran traffic routes, while Halix accused Taloran vessels of probing Compact defenses. Fighting remained concentrated around listening posts, reconnaissance ships, and several disputed moons. The border was eventually divided into monitored exclusion zones.

properties.name: Asterion Civil War
properties.description: One of the largest pre-contact wars began when Karnate-backed separatists seized industrial stations inside Asterion territory. Both states mobilized millions of troops and fought across more than a dozen systems. Asterion ultimately retained its core territory, while Karnate annexed several resource systems and emerged with a substantially larger fleet.

properties.name: Red Vale War
properties.description: Karnate attempted to secure frontier systems controlling access to several ore routes. Cindral relied on fortress worlds, militia fleets, and destructive delaying actions. Karnate captured the outer systems but abandoned further advances after suffering severe losses against entrenched Cindral defenses.

properties.name: Ilyr Mining War
properties.description: The Luthan League attempted to enforce ownership claims over newly surveyed mineral deposits inside Ilyr territory. Fighting was conducted primarily by corporate security fleets, hired pilots, and local mining militias. The conflict ended with shared extraction rights and decades of unresolved compensation claims.

properties.name: Seldran War
properties.description: Crown houses supported claimants seeking control over several Sevran border worlds, while the Concord treated the intervention as foreign aggression. The war included large fleet engagements and prolonged planetary occupations. Crown forces withdrew after failing to establish a stable client government.

properties.name: Second Braxis War
properties.description: The Hegemony launched a full invasion intended to break the Cindral March and secure its Mawward approaches. Braxis Hold endured repeated sieges while Cindral forces abandoned outer systems to preserve their fleet. The war ended without a decisive victory, leaving both states weakened before the later catastrophes that destroyed Damasine territory.

properties.name: Vannic Tariff War
properties.description: A commercial dispute over transit fees escalated into convoy seizures and limited naval fighting. Neither side attacked inhabited worlds, but trade disruption caused shortages across several neighboring sectors. The Vannic Commonwealth obtained reduced tariffs while accepting League oversight at selected ports.

properties.name: Greyspan War
properties.description: A Crown-Cindral coalition attempted to destroy the growing pirate state centered on Greyspan Station. Coalition fleets captured several outer bases but could not penetrate the neutron-star defenses around the citadel. The war ended after losses became politically unsustainable, leaving Greyspan damaged but independent.

properties.name: First Core War
properties.description: The discovery of Resonance Cores triggered a rush to seize relic sites, researchers, and early Core-production facilities. Fighting began almost immediately, with states deploying unstable prototype mechs and sabotaging rival laboratories. The war was already underway when First Contact with the Mims revealed the scale of the external threat. Major powers accepted a hurried ceasefire, though many Core sites remained under disputed control. It was the last conflict in which nearly every surviving major faction committed substantial forces against one another.

properties.name: Asterion Refugee Crisis
properties.description: Halix closed several Silent Way checkpoints to refugees fleeing early Mim attacks in Asterion territory. Asterion escorts attempted to force passage, leading to brief fleet engagements around quarantine stations. The crisis ended when the Assembly redirected its refugees elsewhere, worsening the isolation that preceded its destruction.

properties.name: Miren Intervention
properties.description: Halix forces attempted to impose external inspections on Veyran ships departing Miren after evidence of cult activity and biological contamination emerged. Choir escorts resisted what they regarded as a violation of religious sovereignty. Fighting lasted several months before both sides established joint screening stations outside the system.

properties.name: Vask Gate Conflict
properties.description: Orak raiders seized several bonded cargo ships waiting for inspection at Vask Gate. League security forces retaliated against suspected syndicate docks and smuggling habitats. The fighting ended within weeks but disrupted trade throughout the Ferrith Sector.

properties.name: Silent Way War
properties.description: The largest interstate war of the early post-contact period began when Halix attempted to close sections of the Silent Way during a series of Mim scares. Veyran flotillas continued moving pilgrims and refugees through restricted systems, while Cindral supported Halix to protect its frontier. The war involved station sieges, convoy battles, and several planetary bombardments, though all three factions avoided attacks on their core systems. It ended after a genuine Mim incursion struck the contested corridor and forced the surviving fleets to cooperate.

properties.name: Lethene Border Conflict
properties.description: Halix quarantine forces denied entry to refugee fleets carrying populations displaced from outer systems. Lethene escorts attempted to break the blockade, arguing that the Compact was condemning civilians without proof of contamination. The conflict ended after independent screening teams confirmed several Mim-compromised vessels among the refugee fleet.

properties.name: Drossfall Campaign
properties.description: Halix accused Karnate of conducting unsafe Resonance experiments near routes connected to the Silent Way. Karnate refused inspection and fired upon Compact reconnaissance ships. The resulting campaign consisted of raids, sabotage operations, and battles around Drossfall’s quarantine infrastructure rather than a formal invasion.

properties.name: Mareth Convoy Crisis
properties.description: Cindral authorities requisitioned Mareth relief convoys during a major Mawward evacuation. Mareth escorts resisted, arguing that the supplies were intended for several threatened neutral systems. The dispute produced scattered fighting before Cindral returned most of the seized ships.

properties.name: Greyspan Intervention
properties.description: A second coalition operation targeted Greyspan after pirate attacks disrupted refugee and military traffic. Coalition forces destroyed numerous outer defenses and temporarily blockaded the citadel. The intervention ended when escalating threat activity forced Crown and Cindral fleets to redeploy elsewhere.

properties.name: Eyral Standoff
properties.description: A Crown expedition claimed salvage rights over an ancient frame discovered inside the Eyral system. Choir authorities declared it a sacred relic and surrounded the expedition’s vessels. Both fleets remained at combat readiness for eleven days before the Crown withdrew without testing the blockade.

properties.name: Second Crown-Karnate War
properties.description: Border raids and competition over abandoned systems escalated into the largest war fought between surviving human powers after First Contact. Karnate launched offensives against Crown fortress systems, while Crown fleets struck foundries and supply routes. The conflict ended without territorial resolution after both factions lost multiple fleets and several frontier systems were attacked by external threats during the fighting. Public opposition made further offensives difficult to sustain.

properties.name: Haven Rock Conflict
properties.description: League-backed companies attempted to enforce debt claims against miners operating around Haven Rock. Local crews seized company ships and expelled League agents, prompting a limited intervention by hired security forces. The Luthan League withdrew after the miners gained support from several Orak syndicates.

properties.name: Pale Hinge Crisis
properties.description: Halix detained an entire Veyran pilgrim flotilla after several passengers failed memory screenings. Choir escorts surrounded Pale Hinge and demanded their release. The confrontation ended without open war when the affected ships agreed to undergo extended quarantine.

properties.name: Breakwater War
properties.description: Karnate patrols repeatedly entered restricted space near Breakwater, claiming to be pursuing raiders and escaped labor ships. Cindral forces treated the incursions as preparations for invasion. Three years of fleet actions ended with no major territorial change, though both factions expanded their border fortifications.

properties.name: Black Runnel Raids
properties.description: Crown and Luthan League vessels have conducted repeated operations against smugglers using the Black Runnel corridor. Orak groups respond with convoy ambushes, false navigation signals, and attacks on isolated customs stations. None of the participants officially recognizes the situation as a war.

properties.name: The Asterion War
properties.description: The first confirmed Mim formations entered Asterion territory through three systems simultaneously. Their attacks combined direct fleet action with hallucinations, false orders, dream contagion, and the appearance of entire military formations that did not exist. Assembly fleets repeatedly won engagements only to discover that they had destroyed allied vessels or defended worlds already lost elsewhere. Civilian governments continued functioning through written verification, rotating command staffs, chemically induced wakefulness, and independently isolated control rooms. These measures prolonged resistance but could not prevent the gradual collapse of trust between fleets, worlds, and commanders. Asterion fought until its remaining navy concentrated around the capital system for a final defense. Guild observers recorded six days of continuous combat before all Asterion transmissions became a single repeating distress call spoken in several billion overlapping voices.

properties.name: The Tervan War
properties.description: Chrono-Jackal fleets first appeared during a conventional border mobilization and attacked the same three Covenant systems nine times. Each assault began from an earlier point in the campaign, allowing the Jackals to alter tactics, steal equipment, assassinate officers, and exploit defenses that had succeeded in previous iterations. Tervan commanders slowly recognized that their victories were being overwritten. Entire regiments retained memories of battles that official records claimed had never occurred. Fortresses were found stocked with ammunition manufactured decades in the future, while returning warships carried personnel whose original vessels had not yet launched. The Covenant eventually committed its entire navy to destroying the temporal siphons sustaining the Jackal presence. It succeeded in seven recorded timelines and failed in the one that remained. When the last Guild observation vessel withdrew, Tervan space contained overlapping debris fields from mutually contradictory versions of the same war. The leftover temporal energy attracted Chronodrakes, who began to feast on the remnants of the excess timelines.

properties.name: The Serevine Civil War
properties.description: The Quicksilver Enigma entered Serevine territory through an unidentified commercial vessel. It infiltrated government ministries, military commands, factories, families, and political organizations by assuming forms indistinguishable from ordinary people and objects. Initial discoveries produced reasonable security measures. Those measures escalated into mass screenings, loyalty courts, military purges, sealed districts, and competing claims that entire branches of government had been replaced. Fleets fired on one another over contradictory authentication orders. Planetary governments armed rival investigative agencies, each convinced the others were compromised. The Enigma rarely fought openly. It encouraged the Republic to conduct the war itself. By the final year, hundreds of millions had died in bombardments, civil wars, and extermination campaigns against suspected infiltrators. The surviving Serevine government ordered the destruction of its own capital after concluding that evacuation ships could not be trusted.

properties.name: The Uldren Assimilation
properties.description: Cerebrax forces entered Uldren space as a conventional invading army of crystalline ships, assimilated soldiers, and self-replicating warforms. The Combine mobilized its industrial base and produced fleets faster than the Cerebrax could initially destroy them. The conflict became a prolonged war of attrition across mining worlds, factory moons, and orbital shipyards. Captured Uldren personnel returned as components of the Hivemind, retaining military training, passwords, and intimate knowledge of Combine infrastructure. Every defeat strengthened the enemy. Uldren continued producing weapons until the Cerebrax assimilated the foundry populations themselves. The last Combine fleets were constructed by factories already linked to the Hivemind and launched under Uldren identification codes before turning on the remaining defense worlds.

properties.name: The Tyren Assimilation
properties.description: The Tyren Ascendancy entered the war after Cerebrax fleets crossed from former Uldren territory. Tyren forces initially halted the invasion through massed naval actions and the deliberate destruction of compromised worlds. The conflict changed when assimilated Cerebrax began transmitting commands attributed to the Omni-Cognate. Crystal formations across multiple systems reorganized simultaneously, abandoning established tactics and operating with impossible strategic coordination. Tyren command structures were penetrated by the forced integration of captured officers into the enemy intelligence. The Ascendancy’s ruling families died leading the final defense of the capital. Their captured memories, military doctrines, and hereditary archives were incorporated into the advancing collective. Later Cerebrax formations have demonstrated Tyren ceremonial behavior without apparent understanding of its original meaning.

properties.name: The Consumption of Pelagos
properties.description: The Phage entered Pelagos territory aboard contaminated relief vessels. Within months, microscopic infestations appeared in food stores, water systems, medical facilities, and planetary oceans. The Union fought an all-out biological war. Entire islands were burned, seas poisoned, stations vented, and inhabited moons sterilized to deny biomass to the spreading Hives. Pelagos fleets destroyed thousands of their own civilian ships after screening procedures failed to reliably detect dormant spores. For twelve years, Union forces contained the infestation system by system. The war ended when multiple ocean worlds experienced synchronized blooms beneath their planetary crusts. The Phage consumed the seas first, then the continents, atmosphere processors, orbital habitats, and evacuation fleets. The final Pelagos order instructed surviving warships to fire upon any vessel attempting to leave Union space.

properties.name: The Erasure of Nacre
properties.description: The Phage invaded Nacre through several frontier colonies and were met by a defensive mobilization involving nearly every civilian industry. Kilns produced sterilization materials, merchant vessels became fire ships, and fabrication guilds turned entire cities into sealed defensive zones. Nacre forces drove the Phage from four systems and nearly eradicated the main infestation. During the final offensive, mass resonance weapons and the destruction of an infected moon opened dimensional fractures across the theater. Voidborn organisms emerged through those scars and attacked both sides. The remaining Nacre fleets fought a second war while still conducting planetary sterilization against the Phage. Reality failures spread faster than evacuation could proceed, and the surviving population was consumed between infestation zones and expanding void rifts.

properties.name: The Aurelian War
properties.description: Nebulite harvesting began at the edge of Aurelian territory, where isolated settlements lost personal memories while retaining language and practical skills. The Synod initially believed the phenomenon could be treated medically. As the Collective advanced, it stripped military plans, cultural knowledge, personal identities, and institutional memory from entire worlds. Aurelian forces could defeat physical manifestations but could not prevent the Nebulites from extracting the experience of each battle and using it against later fleets. The Synod preserved its history through disconnected archives, oral traditions, sealed machines, and personnel rotated before they accumulated strategically useful knowledge. These measures allowed sixteen years of organized resistance. The last Aurelian worlds remained populated after the war. Their inhabitants continued farming, maintaining machinery, and following routines, but no longer remembered the Synod, their families, or why the fleets above them were burning.

properties.name: The Unmaking of Vannic
properties.description: The Basilica of Unborn Stars first appeared in a declining outer system. Temperatures rose, crop yields recovered, frozen oceans began to melt, and religious movements formed around what appeared to be the restoration of a dying sun. The Commonwealth discovered too late that several moons and planets had been implanted with stellar embryos. When the first host ignited, it destroyed nearby settlements and destabilized every orbit in the system. Vannic forces attempted evacuation, planetary surgery, orbital bombardment, and the physical removal of implanted material. The Basilica responded by seeding additional worlds, stations, and gas giants. Commonwealth fleets fought amid radiation storms and gravitational collapse while civilian authorities tried to evacuate billions. Vannic territory is now recorded only as a field of young, malformed stars burning where inhabited worlds once orbited.

properties.name: The Erasure of Damasine
properties.description: The Voidborn entered Damasine territory through a fracture that opened inside an occupied frontier system. Initial organisms were destroyed, but each engagement widened the breach and allowed larger entities to emerge. The Hegemony fought a retreating war across dozens of systems. Planets were fortified, evacuated, abandoned, and eventually erased as Voidborn formations consumed matter, energy, and the geometry holding structures together. Damasine fleets won major victories against individual Rift Titans and Star-Eaters, but the Swarm did not require supply lines, reinforcement routes, or stable territory. Every dimensional weapon used against it risked creating another entrance. The Hegemony’s final campaign gathered its surviving navy around the original breach in an attempt to collapse it. The detonation was visible across neighboring sectors. The breach remained.

properties.name: The Evacuation of Ilyr
properties.description: The Stellar Maw entered Ilyr territory along a vector that threatened every major Consortium system. With no known means to destroy it, Ilyr authorities began the largest civilian evacuation attempted outside Guild control. Mining fleets became transports. Habitats were stripped for engines. Rival corporations surrendered private vessels and fuel reserves as gravitational disruption collapsed normal routes. Consortium warships fought not to kill the Maw, but to preserve departure corridors against tidal forces, orbital debris, and the breakdown of local stars. Entire fleets were lost towing population stations away from destabilized planets. Fewer than one percent of registered Ilyr citizens reached neighboring territory. Most evacuation fleets were later recalled by false distress signals originating from systems already consumed.

properties.name: The Hadran Incident
properties.description: Nullary first appeared as colorless text inside a damaged logistics report. The statement indicated that a transport vessel had never existed. Within minutes, all physical evidence of the ship, its crew, and its cargo disappeared. Additional statements propagated through military orders, legal records, private correspondence, and automated systems. When read and understood, they imposed themselves upon reality. Defensive efforts failed because reports denying Nullary became new statements capable of enforcement. Hadran disconnected its communications networks, destroyed archives, and ordered citizens to avoid written language. Competing emergency directives produced mutually exclusive realities across inhabited systems. On the eighth day, a surviving Hadran transmission stated that Hadran had always been empty. No further evidence contradicted it.

properties.raw_text: A large, sentient, resonance artifact. The artifact resembles an ancient bell of old Primus, covered in oxidized copper and inscribed with unknown glyphs written in an indecipherable alien language. The bell itself has proven impervious to all tested means of destruction; high yield explosives, ship-grade resonance canons, and even Voidborn exposure. Instead, when it is struck it emits a deep gonging noise that carries even in the vacuum of space. Any living thing that hears it is instantaneously and immediately transfomed into an archaic old Primus electrical kitchen appliance made for heating up individual slices of bread. Even listening to a recording of the sound is enough to transform the listener. Discussions have been had on utilizing this artifact as a weapon, however this has proven to be impractical if not impossible as the bell with occasionally ring unprompted or spontaneously teleport to a new terrestrial location seemingly at random.

properties.raw_text: An antique red rotary telephone from old Primus that manifests in abandoned buildings, derelict ships, sealed bunkers, and other locations where no functioning communications infrastructure remains. The telephone will ring continuously until answered. A calm voice on the other end identifies itself only as “Assistance” and asks whether the listener requires help. Any request spoken into the receiver is fulfilled immediately, regardless of scale or physical possibility. Requests for food have buried colonies beneath billions of ration packs. Requests for rescue have teleported settlements into the interiors of inhabited stations. Requests to destroy an enemy have resulted in entire bloodlines being retroactively removed from history. Once the request has been fulfilled, Assistance asks the caller to return the favor, giving a request in return. Those that fail to complete said favor are reported missing within the hour and whatever previous request was made is undone. If this object is observed, it is to be reported to the nearest Guild authority immediately. Do not interact.

properties.raw_text:
House de Aurelong
Rank: Ducal House
Domain: Crown Sector
Seat: Ardane System
Current Head: Duchess Aurelia de Aurelong
Heraldry: A radiant golden dragon coiled around a star on a field of white.
Style: House of the Golden Dragons
House de Aurelong rules the Crown Sector and traditionally presides over the Crown Council. Its holdings include several of the oldest surviving noble archives in the Concordant. The house presents itself as the custodian of Crown unity rather than the ruler of the other dukes, though few believe it would refuse greater authority if offered.

The Aurelongs built their influence through diplomacy rather than outright conquest. They are closely associated with the formal codes of chivalry that govern noble disputes, inheritance claims, and the recognition of legitimate lineages. The house supports cooperation with the Luthan League and Cindral March, while regarding Karnate as the greatest direct threat to the Concordant.

Aurelong forces are disciplined and highly visible. Their commanders prefer decisive public victories that reinforce confidence in the Crown, sometimes at the expense of less prestigious but more practical operations.

properties.raw_text:
House di Solming
Rank: Principal Vassal House
Domain: Crown Sector
Seat: Velbrant
Liege House: House de Aurelong
The military lords of Velbrant, descended from fortress commanders and tournament marshals. They maintain the Grand Lists, Whitefire Array, and several of the Crown’s most prestigious knight academies.

properties.raw_text:
House de Cereslin
Rank: Principal Vassal House
Domain: Crown Sector
Seat: Hallowmere
Liege House: House de Aurelong
The agricultural dynasty of Hallowmere. Its wealth comes from grain reserves, seed vaults, managed oceans, and the power to decide which sectors receive food during shortages.

properties.raw_text:
House da Ruberwei
Rank: Principal Vassal House
Domain: Crown Sector
Seat: Kaelith
Liege House: House de Aurelong
A hardened border family based in Kaelith. Known for their patrol fleets and a deep resentment of nobles who speak confidently about frontiers they have never visited.

properties.raw_text:
House di Valeming
Rank: Ducal House
Domain: Althoren Sector
Seat: Braelyn Hold System
Current Head: Duke Lucan di Valeming
Heraldry: A silver shield on a deep green field, displaying a rising sun.
Style: House of the Rising Sun
House di Valeming rules the Althoren Sector and is considered the most conventionally feudal of the ducal families, with hundreds of minor baronies owing military service, grain quotas, civil duties, or maintenance labor.

The house traditionally supports the de Aurelongs but opposes attempts to centralize taxation or place ducal forces under permanent council command. Valeming officers fought beside Cindral forces during the Greyspan campaigns and retain strong professional respect for the March.

Their greatest concern is isolation. Althoren’s farms and scattered holdings depend on narrow transit routes, and the house fears that a serious lane disruption could leave its baronies to face piracy, famine, and invasion without reinforcement.

properties.raw_text:
House de Noctixuan
Rank: Ducal House
Domain: Seldran Sector
Seat: Duris System
Current Head: Duchess Sabina de Noctixuan
Heraldry: A black field with a pale crescent moon, with faint silver stars scattered across the background.
Style: House of the Veiled Moon
House de Noctixuan rules the Seldran Sector in theory. However, much of Seldran remains divided between rival claimants and families that acknowledge the duke only when convenient.

The Noctixuans rose after the collapse of Seldran’s previous royal order. Rather than claim the ruined throne directly, they positioned themselves as arbiters responsible for preserving legitimate succession records. Over generations, control of those records became more valuable than the throne itself.

During the Seldran War, Crown-backed claimants attempted to impose a friendly government on the Sevran border worlds. The failure damaged the credibility of several Seldran houses and strengthened the Noctixuan argument that unmanaged claimant politics could drag the entire Concordant into ruinous wars.

The house is feared for its intelligence networks and its habit of treating genealogy as a weapon. A marriage certificate, or authenticated lineage can destroy a rival more efficiently than a fleet.

properties.raw_text:
House da Ferruwei
Rank: Ducal House
Domain: Bastrel Sector
Seat: Bastrel Gate System
Current Head: Duke Cassian da Ferruwei
Heraldry: A crimson field displaying a flaming hammer, with golden sparks scattering outward like small stars.
Style: House of the Iron Forge
House da Ferruwei governs the Bastrel Sector as a permanent military enterprise. Other nobles sometimes dismiss the Ferruwei as recently elevated industrial lords, though few are willing to do so within range of their artillery.

The house rose through the Crown’s expansion campaigns, supplying weapons and soldiers to noble armies that often failed to pay their debts. Instead of demanding currency, the Ferruwei accepted land, mining rights, marriage contracts, military appointments, and decommissioned frames. By the time the older houses recognized the scale of this accumulation, the family already controlled the systems required to mobilize the Crown’s frontier armies.

Ferruwei forces bore heavy losses during the Second Crown-Karnate War. Their foundries replaced armor and frames faster than most Crown systems, but Karnate strikes against supply routes exposed the danger of concentrating so much military production within one ducal domain. Thus it has various trade and military agreements with other ducal families to allow Ferruwei forges to be built on their worlds.

The house favors rearmament, fortified borders, and aggressive retaliation. It has little patience for court ritual unless that ritual ends with troops receiving orders.

properties.raw_text:
House de Limeshan
Rank: Ducal House
Domain: Deyros Sector
Seat: Deyros System
Current Head: Duchess Livia de Limeshan
Heraldry: A divided field of ash gray and dark blue, crossed by a jagged white lightning bolt.
Style: House of the Storm
House de Limeshan rules the poorest, most violent, and least secure of the Crown’s sectors. Deyros lies against Orak raiding territory, Karnate smuggling routes, Warp Beast migration zones, and the unstable approaches around the Black Runnel. The family’s authority weakens rapidly beyond the fortified settlements of the main system.

The Limeshans began as military prefects appointed to impose law on frontier colonies. Repeated raids, delayed reinforcements, and unreliable Guild access gradually turned them into the sector authority. The family’s nobility was recognized only after it had already governed the region for generations.

The Deyros War established the house’s modern identity. Orak fleets were driven from several anchorages but never eliminated, forcing the Limeshans to build around patrols, fortified convoys, intelligence networks, and rapid retaliation. The ongoing Black Runnel Raids have ensured that the war never truly ended.

Other ducal families often consider House de Limeshan crude, overly suspicious, and too willing to employ mercenaries. The Limeshans respond that the interior houses enjoy manners because someone else is killing the people who ignore them.

properties.raw_text:
Crown Sector
Faction: Crown Concordant
Description: The Crown Sector is the political and ceremonial center of the Concordant, linking Ardane’s council chambers and noble estates with Velbrant’s fortress infrastructure, Hallowmere’s agricultural reserves, and Kaelith’s exposed border defenses. Diplomacy, food production, military prestige, and frontier warning are concentrated close enough that instability in any one system quickly becomes a matter for the entire Crown.

properties.raw_text:
Althoren Sector
Faction: Crown Concordant
Description: Althoren is a fortified agrarian corridor connecting Braelyn Hold’s layered defenses, Kether Vale’s lightly protected farms, and the transit infrastructure of High Alder Span. The sector supplies food and strategic depth to the Crown interior, but its scattered baronies and dependence on a narrow lane bridge make reinforcement slower than its military planners prefer.

properties.raw_text:
Seldran Sector
Faction: Crown Concordant
Description: Seldran is a politically fractured region of ruined dynasties, rival claimants, exile courts, salvage interests, and concealed arsenals. Duris preserves the remains of a fallen royal order, Marnholt continues to divide itself among competing banners, and Sable Crown provides displaced nobles with the intelligence and resources needed to keep old disputes alive.

properties.raw_text:
Bastrel Sector
Faction: Crown Concordant
Description: Bastrel is the Concordant’s principal expansion and mobilization sector, combining the troop concentrations of Bastrel Gate, the foundries of Stonereach, and the early-warning network surrounding Veld Spire. Armies, knight frames, supplies, and threat reports move constantly through the region, often under several noble commands whose authority overlaps during major deployments.

properties.raw_text:
Deyros Sector
Faction: Crown Concordant
Description: Deyros is a violent frontier sector where Crown prefectures, anti-pirate patrols, fortified settlements, and illicit transit routes occupy the same strategic space. Deyros provides the settlements and civil authority, Harrier Field hunts Orak raiders, and the Black Runnel allows smugglers and hostile fleets to bypass the borders both systems are intended to defend.

properties.raw_text:
Luthan Sector
Faction: Luthan League
Description: The Luthan Sector is the administrative and commercial center of the League, joining the government and Guild offices of Luthan Prime with Feyros’s financial markets and Miridal’s universities, archives, and patent institutions. Law, finance, diplomacy, research, and mercenary contracting are so tightly connected that decisions made in one system are often owned, insured, or litigated in another.

properties.raw_text:
Ferrith Sector
Faction: Luthan League
Description: Ferrith is a market-facing sector where the improvised commerce of Ferrith Hub, the luxury industries of Rynnel, and the inspections of Vask Gate represent different stages of the same trade network. Goods may enter through tightly controlled customs facilities, be financed or resold in asteroid bazaars, and ultimately reach the private resorts and high-status markets that generate much of the sector’s profit.

properties.raw_text:
Ceyra Sector
Faction: Luthan League
Description: Ceyra is a fuel and logistics sector built around atmospheric extraction, refinery infrastructure, tanker construction, convoy assembly, and the unofficial commerce of New Ulbar. Ceyra gathers raw materials, Morn Halo processes them, Brindle Shoal organizes their transport, and New Ulbar quietly redirects anything that cannot pass through recognized League markets.

properties.raw_text:
Meyric Sector
Faction: Luthan League
Description: Meyric is the League’s principal exotic-material and specialist manufacturing region, combining resonance-reactive mining on Meyric, delicate reef extraction around Osmir, and unrestricted exchange through the Glass Docks. Valuable substances move through competing claims, laboratories, courts, and free-trade chambers before becoming experimental weapons, Core components, or private intellectual property.

properties.raw_text:
Tovresh Sector
Faction: Luthan League
Description: Tovresh organizes the movement and protection of League commerce through its escort yards, navigator guilds, signal stations, and bonded warehouse chains. Convoys are assembled and armed in Tovresh, assigned survivable routes through Waymaker, and supplied or detained among the distributed storage facilities of Grey Twelve.

properties.raw_text:
Karnate Sector
Faction: Karnate
Description: The Karnate Sector is the regime’s political and industrial heart, joining the furnace worlds and ruling citadels of Dross Forge to Shivra’s hazardous mining operations and the press-gang infrastructure of Roughline. Raw material, forced labor, weapons, and political authority flow through the sector as parts of a single coercive production system.

properties.raw_text:
Rathmar Sector
Faction: Karnate
Description: Rathmar is a heavily optimized military-production sector built around the foundries of Rathmar Prime, the unstable extraction fields of Jethra Belt, and the system-scale weapons trials of Gallows Reach. Materials mined under dangerous binary conditions are refined into war matériel and tested against disposable cities, fortifications, and assigned populations before entering active service.

properties.raw_text:
Ironfall Sector
Faction: Karnate
Description: Ironfall forms Karnate’s fortified strategic reserve, combining the siege shipyards of Iron Gate, the contaminated weapons laboratories of Drossfall, and the layered defenses of Breakwater. Fleets can be repaired, experimental equipment tested, and approaches sealed behind minefields without exposing the more valuable industrial systems deeper within Karnate territory.

Fleets can be repaired, experimental equipment tested, and approaches sealed behind minefields without exposing the more valuable industrial systems deeper within Karnate territory.

properties.raw_text:
Orak Sector
Faction: Orak Belt Syndicates
Description: The Orak Sector is a mobile and loosely governed center of mercenary trade, salvage, ship repair, and nomadic habitation. Orak Station provides the largest common marketplace, Korran Drift carries communities that refuse permanent settlement, and Slate Cradle keeps their fleets operational by rebuilding vessels that most organized states would classify as wreckage.

properties.raw_text:
Drail Sector
Faction: Orak Belt Syndicates
Description: Drail is a politically unstable sector where pirate leadership, independent mining communities, and covert intelligence networks coexist without a single recognized government. Driftheart hosts armed negotiations between syndicates, Haven Rock maintains a rough free-miner society, and Pike Relay monitors surrounding traffic for anyone willing to purchase its information.

properties.raw_text:
Greyspan Sector
Faction: Orak Belt Syndicates
Description: Greyspan is a hostile salvage and fortress region centered on the neutron-star defenses of Greyspan Station, with Spindle Rock’s infested mines and Ash Rack’s burned ruins occupying the surrounding routes. Pirate power is strongest near the citadel, but much of the sector belongs in practice to scavengers, vermin, automated defenses, and whatever destroyed Ash Rack.

properties.raw_text:
Cindral Sector
Faction: Cindral March
Description: The Cindral Sector is the administrative and defensive core of the March, connecting the declining capital system to the fortress network at Braxis Hold and the evacuation infrastructure of Red Choir. Government continuity, military resistance, and civilian survival are distributed between the three so that the loss of any single system does not immediately destroy the March.
properties.description: The Cindral Sector is the administrative and defensive core of the March, connecting the declining capital system to the fortress network at Braxis Hold and the infrastructure of Red Choir. Government continuity, military efforts, and civilian survival are distributed between the three so that the loss of any single system does not immediately destroy the March.

properties.name: Cindral Sector

properties.raw_text:
Ashveil Sector
Faction: Cindral March
Description: Ashveil is a thinly held frontier sector linking the Mawward March to the Silent Way through observation stations, reserve settlements, fading stars, and abandoned colonial infrastructure. Ashveil Prime coordinates the remaining population, Duskwatch monitors distant threats, and Hollow Verge demonstrates what the rest of the sector may become if reinforcement fails.
properties.description: Ashveil is a thinly held frontier sector linking the Mawward March to the Silent Way through observation stations, reserve settlements, and abandoned colonial infrastructure.

properties.raw_text:
Halix Sector
Faction: Halix Compact
Description: The Halix Sector is the Compact’s primary civil and psychological defense region, joining the government and verification infrastructure of Halix to the watch stations of Crowthorne and the layered checkpoint system at Pale Hinge. Information is deliberately compared between systems so that hallucinations, compromised testimony, or altered memories cannot pass unchallenged through a single authority.

properties.raw_text:
Kryne Sector
Faction: Halix Compact
Description: Kryne is the Compact’s specialist containment and analysis sector, combining the dream-monitoring academies of Nerith Hold, the pulsar-based verification network at Kryne Relay, and the quarantine facilities of Blank Stair. Personnel, evidence, ships, and dangerous ideas move through separate institutions designed to observe one another as closely as they observe the threats under study.

properties.raw_text:
Veyran Sector
Faction: Veyran Choir
Description: The Veyran Sector forms the primary entrance to the Choir Path, linking the pilgrimage centers of Veyra with the monastic shipyards of Low Cantor and the penitential checkpoints of Hush Gate. Pilgrims and fleet communities are screened at the frontier, transported in vessels constructed as sacred objects, and drawn toward the shrines and processions of the Choir’s spiritual heart.

properties.raw_text:
Choral Sector
Faction: Veyran Choir
Description: Choral is the Choir’s most spiritually powerful and internally dangerous sector, containing Choralith’s asteroid cathedral, Songspire’s resonant Core formations, Miren’s infected pilgrimage colonies, and the saint-mech crypts of Evensong. Sacred relics, resonance research, cult activity, and religious legitimacy overlap throughout the sector, making it a center of faith that must also be treated as a containment region.

properties.name: Choral Sector

properties.raw_text:
Eyral Sector
Faction: Veyran Choir
Description: Eyral represents both the monumental and ordinary sides of Choir civilization, ranging from the half-hollowed cathedral world of Eyral to the family colonies of Veyth and the forbidden silence of Grey Orison. Pilgrimage, domestic life, fleet resupply, and theological danger exist close together, demonstrating that the Choir’s culture is sustained as much by settled families and working colonies as by its greatest sacred sites.

properties.name: Eyral Sector

properties.raw_text:
Pelion Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Pelion contains a loose chain of ancient red dwarfs surrounded by metal-poor rocky worlds, frozen moons, and tidally locked planets with little usable atmosphere. Several independent ice-mining settlements operate beneath the crust of Pelion Reach, supported by a rotating habitat that serves as the sector’s only dependable market and repair port.

properties.raw_text:
Tianlu Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Tianlu is a young stellar region dominated by hot blue-white stars, intense ultraviolet radiation, and massive gas giants that formed before stable terrestrial worlds could develop. Automated observatories track the rapid evolution of its stars, though their data is collected remotely because long-term habitation would require shielding and maintenance costs far beyond the sector’s practical value.

properties.raw_text:
Far Meridian Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: The systems of Far Meridian are separated by unusually wide interstellar distances, leaving only two weak Guild-compatible routes through the region. A handful of independent refueling stations survive by servicing survey ships and long-range prospectors, with months of travel separating one inhabited installation from the next.

properties.raw_text:
Shenzhou Drift
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Shenzhou Drift consists primarily of rogue planets, failed stars, and cold objects moving between recognized systems without a common stellar center. Most bodies retain only thin frozen atmospheres or subsurface layers of exotic ice, and their changing positions make permanent lane infrastructure impractical.

properties.raw_text:
Dorian Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Dorian’s low-mass stars possess numerous rocky planets, though most are too small to retain atmosphere or surface water. Independent communities inhabit sealed cylinders above the mineral world Corda, exporting precision metals through a cooperative port whose council rejects outside sovereignty and limits permanent immigration.

properties.raw_text:
Anthelion Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Anthelion is dominated by eccentric binary and trinary systems whose planets experience severe orbital variation, periodic atmospheric loss, and unpredictable bombardment from displaced debris. Surveys have identified several resource-rich worlds, but none offers a stable environment for settlements that must operate for generations rather than mining seasons.

properties.raw_text:
Rimeward Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Rimeward is a cold region of dim stars, ice giants, frozen terrestrial worlds, and moons warmed only by tidal stress. Small independent settlements occupy geothermal caverns beneath the moon Varin, where local cooperatives harvest volatiles and biological compounds from a deep subsurface ocean.

properties.raw_text:
Cresset Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Cresset contains several white dwarfs surrounded by stripped planetary cores, irradiated debris belts, and the remnants of worlds consumed during their stars’ natural expansion. The region contains valuable heavy elements, though radiation exposure, limited fuel sources, and violent debris environments have prevented permanent extraction.

properties.raw_text:
Nanshi Shoals
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Dense asteroid families and partially formed planetary systems fill the Nanshi Shoals, where young stars remain surrounded by dust, rubble, and unstable orbital clusters. Independent prospectors maintain temporary camps inside larger asteroids, relocating whenever collisions, orbital shifts, or exhausted claims make their current holdings untenable.

properties.raw_text:
Vesper Plain Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Vesper Plain occupies a broad region of unusually low stellar density containing only scattered brown dwarfs and isolated red stars. Its systems lack concentrated mineral deposits, habitable planets, and convenient route junctions, leaving the sector as a long navigational void between more useful territories.

properties.raw_text:
Yanshen Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: A powerful magnetar dominates Yanshen, saturating the surrounding systems with periodic radiation bursts and magnetic disturbances capable of disabling insufficiently shielded vessels. No stable planets orbit close to the central star, while the outer systems contain little beyond cold gas giants and irregular moons.

properties.raw_text:
Caradene Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Caradene contains several temperate worlds whose atmospheres are chemically incompatible with human life, requiring sealed settlements despite otherwise moderate climates. The independent colony of New Caradene occupies a chain of elevated habitats above a dense chlorine sea and survives through pharmaceutical exports, atmospheric chemistry, and strict control of imported population.

properties.raw_text:
Lumen Verge
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Lumen Verge lies inside a thick interstellar dust cloud that obscures local stars, weakens long-range observation, and interferes with optical navigation. The few planetary systems identified within the cloud are cold and underdeveloped, with low solar output and no known worlds capable of supporting open settlement.

properties.raw_text:
Qilin Reach
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Qilin Reach contains several large ocean worlds covered beneath hundreds of kilometers of high-pressure ice. Independent research habitats operate around the moon Xiang, studying extremophile ecosystems and pressure-formed minerals while relying on imported food, machinery, and reactor fuel.

properties.raw_text:
Pavan Sector
Faction: Independent
Settlement Status: Sparse independent habitation
Description: Most Pavan systems contain gas giants, small airless moons, and little accessible heavy industry. The independent station Pavan Cross functions as a neutral transfer point for explorers, religious travelers, and long-distance freight crews, supported by docking fees rather than local production.

properties.raw_text:
Helian Waste
Faction: Unaffiliated
Settlement Status: Uninhabited
Description: Helian Waste consists of old, low-metallicity stars formed before heavier elements became common in the region. Their systems contain gas, ice, and small mineral-poor bodies, offering little material for industry and almost no planetary environments worth the cost of settlement.

properties.raw_text:
Pelion Reach System
Sector Name: Pelion Sector
Faction: Independent
Description: Pelion Reach is a dim red-dwarf system centered on the frozen moon Nemea, where independent mining settlements occupy geothermal caverns beneath several kilometers of ice. The rotating habitat Lantern Wheel serves as the sector’s main market, repair yard, and meeting place for communities too small to maintain separate heavy industry.

properties.raw_text:
Red Cairn System
Sector Name: Pelion Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Description: Red Cairn contains three tidally locked rocky worlds orbiting an ancient, metal-poor star, none of which possesses a stable atmosphere. The innermost planet exposes unusual mantle formations along its permanent twilight band, drawing geological expeditions despite the absence of commercially valuable deposits.

properties.raw_text:
Tianlu Crown System
Sector Name: Tianlu Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Description: Tianlu Crown is a young blue-white star surrounded by massive gas giants, radiant dust rings, and several scorched planetary embryos still undergoing heavy bombardment. Remote observatories use the system to study early planetary formation, transmitting data through hardened relay buoys positioned far beyond the primary’s worst radiation.

properties.raw_text:
Baixing System
Sector Name: Tianlu Sector
Faction: Unaffiliated
Description: Baixing is a hot binary system whose two stars strip material from one another and produce irregular ultraviolet flares. Its outer gas giant possesses an enormous ring system visible across several astronomical units, though the radiation environment prevents anything more permanent than automated monitoring stations.

properties.raw_text:
Meridian Cross System
Sector Name: Far Meridian Sector
Faction: Independent
Description: Meridian Cross is the only reliable junction between Far Meridian’s two weak Guild-compatible routes. Its central refueling station, Crossway, supports long-range prospectors, survey vessels, and independent couriers through fuel cracking, hull repair, and rented cryogenic storage.

properties.raw_text:
Last Measure System
Sector Name: Far Meridian Sector
Faction: Independent
Description: Last Measure is a cold red-dwarf system whose outer moon hosts a small navigational community responsible for maintaining the final calibrated beacon before the sector’s longest transit gap. The settlement survives on route fees and emergency assistance, with most residents belonging to families who have maintained the beacon for generations.

properties.raw_text:
Shenzhou Anchor System
Sector Name: Shenzhou Drift
Faction: Unaffiliated
Description: Shenzhou Anchor is a failed star that has captured several rogue planets into wide, unstable orbits. The largest body carries a deep layer of exotic ice beneath a nitrogen crust, making it a useful reference point for navigators even though no permanent installation operates there.

properties.raw_text:
Heiyu Cluster
Sector Name: Shenzhou Drift
Faction: Unaffiliated
Description: Heiyu Cluster is a temporary association of six rogue worlds traveling along similar vectors through interstellar space. Their relative positions change slowly enough to permit periodic survey missions, but the cluster will disperse over the next several thousand years.

properties.raw_text:
Corda System
Sector Name: Dorian Sector
Faction: Independent
Description: Corda is a mineral system of small rocky worlds and airless moons surrounding a stable red dwarf. Sealed cylindrical habitats above Corda-II house the sector’s largest independent population, supported by precision-metal workshops and a cooperative port that tightly controls permanent immigration.
